$schema: Each example: studentQuestion + mentorResponse the Profiler would see, plus expected fields the eval scorer compares against. Concept tags are canonical (see frontend/src/lib/ai/concept-canon.ts). Some examples are legitimately ambiguous between two canonical siblings (e.g. the gym/timing question maps to BOTH accrual_vs_cash AND revenue_recognition); those use expectedConceptTags (array) and the scorer counts a hit if any listed tag matches. Single-tag examples keep expectedConceptTag (string) and remain strict. Misconception types are coarse buckets — the scorer counts a hit when ≥2 content tokens (length ≥4) from the labeled phrase appear anywhere in the Profiler's misconception+bottleneck text.

examples.studentQuestion: Wait, the gym got the $1,200 in January. Isn't that January's revenue?
examples.mentorResponse: Let me push back gently — what triggers revenue under accrual accounting: when the cash arrives, or when the service is delivered?

examples.studentQuestion: So if I run a consulting firm and bill in December but get paid in January, that's December revenue?
examples.mentorResponse: Exactly — you've got the timing trigger. Now what would the matching principle say about the costs you incurred to deliver that work?

examples.studentQuestion: Why would I expense the salesperson's commission in March if the deal closed in January?
examples.mentorResponse: Walk me through it — what was the commission *for*, and which month earned the revenue it generated?
examples.expectedMisconceptionType: treating cost as period-of-payment rather than period-of-benefit

examples.studentQuestion: Got it — the cost rides with the revenue it produced, not when it was paid.
examples.mentorResponse: Good. To check that with a fresh case: a factory pays its electricity bill in February for power consumed in January. Which month does the expense belong to?

examples.studentQuestion: If I pay $12K for a year of insurance in January, isn't that all a January expense?
examples.mentorResponse: If the insurance protects the whole year, what does the matching principle say about how much has been *used up* in January?
examples.expectedMisconceptionType: expensing the full prepaid asset on payment date

examples.studentQuestion: OK so January expenses $1,000, and the other $11,000 sits as a prepaid asset?
examples.mentorResponse: Exactly. And what happens to that asset balance month after month?

examples.studentQuestion: The gym got $1,200 today for a 12-month membership. Why isn't that all revenue today?
examples.mentorResponse: Take it slowly — what does the gym actually owe in exchange for that $1,200?
examples.expectedMisconceptionType: confusing cash receipt with earned revenue

examples.studentQuestion: So $100 a month for 12 months, and the rest is a liability called unearned revenue?
examples.mentorResponse: Right. Now show me the journal entry on day one.

examples.studentQuestion: Why does the rent expense show in March if I prepaid all of Q1 in January?
examples.mentorResponse: Think about *consumed* vs *paid*. Which one drives the expense recognition?
examples.expectedMisconceptionType: expense recognition based on cash timing rather than consumption

examples.studentQuestion: I think I get it — I expense one third of the prepayment each month as the benefit is consumed.
examples.mentorResponse: Good. And what's left on the balance sheet after month one?

examples.studentQuestion: If the company borrows $10K, do assets and equity both go up?
examples.mentorResponse: Pause on that — borrowing means the company *owes* someone. Which side of the equation does that land on?

examples.studentQuestion: Right — assets up, liabilities up, equity unchanged.
examples.mentorResponse: Exactly. Walk me through what happens when the company repays $2K of that loan.

examples.studentQuestion: We bought a $50K truck. Why don't we just expense $50K this year?
examples.mentorResponse: How long do you expect that truck to be doing work for the business?
examples.expectedMisconceptionType: expensing capitalized asset in year of purchase

examples.studentQuestion: So if it's useful for 5 years, $10K a year as depreciation expense.
examples.mentorResponse: Right. And how does that show up on the balance sheet?

examples.studentQuestion: Is a one-year service contract we just signed an asset?
examples.mentorResponse: What's the test for an asset — has the company already given something up to receive a future benefit?
examples.expectedMisconceptionType: treating contractual rights as recognized assets without past consideration

examples.studentQuestion: Why would a company with cheap debt still have a high WACC?
examples.mentorResponse: Walk me through the formula — what else feeds into WACC besides the cost of debt?
examples.expectedMisconceptionType: treating WACC as the cost of debt only, ignoring equity weighting

examples.studentQuestion: Our CAC is just total ad spend divided by new customers, right?
examples.mentorResponse: Hmm — what about the salaries of the sales team and the tooling you use to run the funnel?
examples.expectedMisconceptionType: equating acquisition cost with ad spend only, excluding overhead

examples.studentQuestion: So fully loaded — ads + sales salaries + tooling + commissions, divided by net new customers acquired in the period.
examples.mentorResponse: Exactly. What does that change about how you compare CAC to LTV?

examples.studentQuestion: If a customer pays $50/month, isn't LTV just $50 times however many months they stay?
examples.mentorResponse: What about the cost to actually serve them — does the gross margin matter to LTV?
examples.expectedMisconceptionType: computing LTV on revenue, not gross margin

examples.studentQuestion: OK so LTV = (ARPU × gross margin %) / churn. And then I compare that to CAC.
examples.mentorResponse: Right. And what LTV-to-CAC ratio would tell you the business is healthy?
